title: Don't Start ANY Claude Code Project Until You Watch This
summary: Austin Marchese presents three rules for successfully building projects with Claude Code, drawn from Y Combinator CEO Garry Tan's principles. The video outlines what not to build, what to build, and six leadership moves for managing an AI team, with a four-question test framework for vetting projects before starting.
writeup: Austin (former $25M startup COO) distills Y Combinator CEO Garry Tan's public guidance into three rules for building AI products: avoid the idea trap by knowing your user precisely and staying out of the frontier labs' direct path; build where you have deep domain judgment rather than surface AI skills, because evaluation ability (knowing good from bad model output) is the actual moat; and shift from execution to orchestration, treating Claude Code and specialized models as a team you direct rather than tools you operate. The through-line is that technical parity is table stakes and the competitive edge now lives in domain depth and leadership clarity.
key_points: The 'idea trap' has two failure modes: not knowing who the user actually is, and building directly against what frontier labs (Anthropic, OpenAI) are already solving at scale, which Tan calls 'picking up a penny in front of a steamroller.'

Garry Tan frames evals, knowing what makes a model's output good versus bad, as the real moat for startups; 49.7% of AI tools being built concentrate in one category while healthcare (1%), legal (<1%), and education (<2%) remain wide open.

Domain expertise is valuable not because AI cannot replicate surface knowledge (it can), but because the judgment layer, distinguishing what actually works and fails in a given field, cannot be prompted into existence; Tan calls this the founder-as-ethnographer stance.

Operating at the leadership layer means giving AI agents onboarding context via a claude.md file, having AI interview you before any build to surface requirements, and granting reversible-action permissions automatically while requiring confirmation only for destructive operations.

Specialized AI 'employees' trained on a specific domain (sales playbook, content strategy, finances) consistently outperform a single generalist; the review pattern is to have AI generate volume and let the human pick the winner, not delegate end-to-end decisions.

Non-technical founder Nick at BDGE used Claude Code under Austin's framework to launch an app generating over $400K per year in roughly 45 days, out-pacing his own full-time engineering team by directing AI better, not by writing more code.
